Formulas

Leibniz

f′(x)=dydx≈ΔyΔx=f(x+Δx)−f(x)Δx∴  Δy=f(x+Δx)−f(x)dy=f′(x)dx\begin{aligned} f'(x)&=\frac{dy}{dx}\approx\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}=\frac{f(x+\Delta x)-f(x)}{\Delta x}\\ \therefore\;\Delta y&=f(x+\Delta x)-f(x)\\ dy&=f'(x)dx\\ \end{aligned}f′(x)∴Δydy​=dxdy​≈ΔxΔy​=Δxf(x+Δx)−f(x)​=f(x+Δx)−f(x)=f′(x)dx​

Linearisation

L(x)=b+y′(a,b)(x−a)b=y(a)L(x)=b+y'(a,b)(x-a)\quad b=y(a) L(x)=b+y′(a,b)(x−a)b=y(a)

  • approx 16.1\sqrt{16.1}16.1​

    (x)′=lim⁡h→0x+h−xh=lim⁡h→0x+h−xh(x+h+x)=12x∴  L(x)=a+12a(x−a)let a=16∴  L(16.1)=4+18(16.1−16)=4.0125(\sqrt{x})'=\lim\limits_{h\to0}\frac{\sqrt{x+h}-\sqrt x}h=\lim\limits_{h\to0}\frac{x+h-x}{h(\sqrt{x+h}+\sqrt x)}=\frac1{2\sqrt x}\\ \therefore\;L(x)=\sqrt a+\frac1{2\sqrt a}(x-a)\\ \text{let }a=16\\ \therefore\;L(16.1)=4+\frac18(16.1-16)=4.0125(x​)′=h→0lim​hx+h​−x​​=h→0lim​h(x+h​+x​)x+h−x​=2x​1​∴L(x)=a​+2a​1​(x−a)let a=16∴L(16.1)=4+81​(16.1−16)=4.0125

Newton

xn+1=xn−f(xn)f′(xn)x_{n+1}=x_n-\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} xn+1​=xn​−f′(xn​)f(xn​)​

approx root of f(x)=x3−2x−5f(x)=x^3-2x-5f(x)=x3−2x−5 (3 decimals)

  1. IVT

    ∵  f(1)=−6<0f(3)=15>0∴  let x0=2∈(1,3)\begin{aligned} \because\;&f(1)=-6<0\quad f(3)=15>0\\ \therefore\;&\text{let }x_0=2\in(1,3) \end{aligned}∵∴​f(1)=−6<0f(3)=15>0let x0​=2∈(1,3)​

  2. find derivative

    f′=lim⁡h→0f(x+h)−f(x)h=...=3x2−2f'=\lim\limits_{h\to0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}h=...=3x^2-2 f′=h→0lim​hf(x+h)−f(x)​=...=3x2−2

  3. iterate (need to find same decimals twice)

    x1=x0−f(x0)f′(x0)=...≈2.1000x2=x1−f(x1)f′(x1)=...≈2.0946x3=x2−f(x2)f′(x2)=...≈2.0946\begin{aligned} &x_1=x_0-\frac{f(x_0)}{f'(x_0)}=...\approx2.1000\\ &x_2=x_1-\frac{f(x_1)}{f'(x_1)}=...\approx2.0946\\ &x_3=x_2-\frac{f(x_2)}{f'(x_2)}=...\approx\textcolor{aqua}{2.0946} \end{aligned}​x1​=x0​−f′(x0​)f(x0​)​=...≈2.1000x2​=x1​−f′(x1​)f(x1​)​=...≈2.0946x3​=x2​−f′(x2​)f(x2​)​=...≈2.0946​

Caution

  1. make sure IVT works
  2. to choose x0x_0x0​ for cos⁡x−x=0\cos x-x=0cosx−x=0
    • cos⁡x=x\cos x=xcosx=x
    • ∵\because∵ intersection in (0,π/2)(0,\pi/2)(0,π/2)
    • ∴\therefore∴ choose x0=1∈(0,π/2)x_0=1\in(0,\pi/2)x0​=1∈(0,π/2)

Deriv

(sin⁡x)′=cos⁡x(cos⁡x)′=−sin⁡x(tan⁡x)′=sec⁡2x(csc⁡x)′=−csc⁡xcot⁡x(sec⁡x)′=sec⁡xtan⁡x(cot⁡x)′=−csc⁡2x\begin{aligned} (\sin x)'&=\cos x&& (\cos x)'=-\sin x&&(\tan x)'=\sec^2x\\ (\csc x)'&=-\csc x\cot x&&(\sec x)'=\sec x\tan x&&(\cot x)'=-\csc^2x\\ \end{aligned}(sinx)′(cscx)′​=cosx=−cscxcotx​​(cosx)′=−sinx(secx)′=secxtanx​​(tanx)′=sec2x(cotx)′=−csc2x​

(asin u)′=u′1−u2(atan u)′=u′1+u2(asec u)′=u′∣u∣u2−1(acos u)′=−(asin u)′(acot u)′=−(atan u)′(acsc u)′=−(asec u)′\begin{aligned} (\text{asin }u)'&=\frac{u'}{\sqrt{1-u^2}}&&(\text{atan }u)'=\frac{u'}{1+u^2}&&(\text{asec }u)'=\frac{u'}{|u|\sqrt{u^2-1}}\\ (\text{acos }u)'&=-(\text{asin }u)'&&(\text{acot }u)'=-(\text{atan }u)'&&(\text{acsc }u)'=-(\text{asec }u)' \end{aligned}(asin u)′(acos u)′​=1−u2​u′​=−(asin u)′​​(atan u)′=1+u2u′​(acot u)′=−(atan u)′​​(asec u)′=∣u∣u2−1​u′​(acsc u)′=−(asec u)′​

sinh⁡(x)=ex−e−x2cosh⁡(x)=ex+e−x2tanh⁡(x)=sinh⁡(x)cosh⁡(x)cosh⁡2x−sinh⁡2x=1 (sinh⁡x)′=cosh⁡(x)(csch x)′=−csch (x) coth(x)(cosh⁡x)′=sinh⁡(x)(sech x)′=−sech (x)tanh⁡(x)(tanh⁡x)′=sech2(x)(coth x)′=−csch2(x)\sinh(x)=\frac{e^x-e^{-x}}2\quad\cosh(x)=\frac{e^x+e^{-x}}2\quad\tanh(x)=\frac{\sinh(x)}{\cosh(x)}\\ \cosh^2x-\sinh^2x=1\\\,\\ \begin{aligned} (\sinh x)'&=\cosh(x)&&(\text{csch }x)'=-\text{csch }(x)\text{ coth}(x)\\ (\cosh x)'&=\sinh(x)&&(\text{sech }x)'=-\text{sech }(x)\tanh(x)\\ (\tanh x)'&=\text{sech}^2(x)&&(\text{coth }x)'=-\text{csch}^2(x)\\ \end{aligned}sinh(x)=2ex−e−x​cosh(x)=2ex+e−x​tanh(x)=cosh(x)sinh(x)​cosh2x−sinh2x=1(sinhx)′(coshx)′(tanhx)′​=cosh(x)=sinh(x)=sech2(x)​​(csch x)′=−csch (x) coth(x)(sech x)′=−sech (x)tanh(x)(coth x)′=−csch2(x)​

(asinh u)′=u′u2+1(acosh u)′=u′u2−1(atanh u)′=u′1−u2(\text{asinh }u)'=\frac{u'}{\sqrt{u^2+1}}\quad(\text{acosh }u)'=\frac{u'}{\sqrt{u^2-1}}\quad(\text{atanh }u)'=\frac{u'}{1-u^2} (asinh u)′=u2+1​u′​(acosh u)′=u2−1​u′​(atanh u)′=1−u2u′​

Hyperbolic Algebra

  • asinh x=ln⁡(x+x2+1)\text{asinh }x=\ln(x+\sqrt{x^2+1})asinh x=ln(x+x2+1​)

    asinh x=y  ⟹  x=sinh⁡(y)=ey−e−y22x=ey−e−yey−2x−e−y=0e2y−2xey−1=0ey=2x±4x2+42=x±x2+1∵  ey>0  ⟹  x±x2+1>0x<x2+1∴  ey=x+x2+1y=ln⁡(x+x2+1)\begin{aligned} \text{asinh }x=y\implies x&=\sinh(y)=\frac{e^y-e^{-y}}2\\ 2x&=e^y-e^{-y}\\ e^y-2x-e^{-y}&=0\\ e^{2y}-2xe^y-1&=0\\ e^y&=\frac{2x\pm\sqrt{4x^2+4}}2=x\pm\sqrt{x^2+1}\\ \because\;e^y&>0\implies x\pm\sqrt{x^2+1}>0\\ x&<\sqrt{x^2+1}\\ \therefore\;e^y&=x+\sqrt{x^2+1}\\ y&=\ln(x+\sqrt{x^2+1}) \end{aligned}asinh x=y⟹x2xey−2x−e−ye2y−2xey−1ey∵eyx∴eyy​=sinh(y)=2ey−e−y​=ey−e−y=0=0=22x±4x2+4​​=x±x2+1​>0⟹x±x2+1​>0<x2+1​=x+x2+1​=ln(x+x2+1​)​

  • if x=ln⁡(sec⁡θ+tan⁡θ)x=\ln(\sec\theta+\tan\theta)x=ln(secθ+tanθ) then sec⁡θ=cosh⁡x\sec\theta=\cosh xsecθ=coshx

    cosh⁡x=ex+e−x2=12(eln⁡(sec⁡θ+tan⁡θ)+e−ln⁡(sec⁡θ+tan⁡θ))=12(sec⁡θ+tan⁡θ+1sec⁡θ+tan⁡θ)=12(sec⁡θ+tan⁡θ+sec⁡θ−tan⁡θsec⁡2θ−tan⁡2θ)=12(sec⁡θ+tan⁡θ+sec⁡θ−tan⁡θ)=sec⁡θ\begin{aligned} \cosh x&=\frac{e^x+e^{-x}}2=\frac12\left(e^{\ln(\sec\theta+\tan\theta)}+e^{-\ln(\sec\theta+\tan\theta)}\right)\\ &=\frac12\left(\sec\theta+\tan\theta+\frac1{\sec\theta+\tan\theta}\right)\\ &=\frac12\left(\sec\theta+\tan\theta+\frac{\sec\theta-\tan\theta}{\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta}\right)\\ &=\frac12(\sec\theta+\tan\theta+\sec\theta-\tan\theta)\\ &=\sec\theta \end{aligned}coshx​=2ex+e−x​=21​(eln(secθ+tanθ)+e−ln(secθ+tanθ))=21​(secθ+tanθ+secθ+tanθ1​)=21​(secθ+tanθ+sec2θ−tan2θsecθ−tanθ​)=21​(secθ+tanθ+secθ−tanθ)=secθ​

Curve Sketching

  1. Dom(f)\text{Dom}(f)Dom(f)
  2. intercepts
    • when x=0x=0x=0, find f(x)f(x)f(x)
    • when f(x)=0f(x)=0f(x)=0 find xxx
  3. symmetry
    • check if f(−x)=f(x)f(-x)=f(x)f(−x)=f(x) or f(−x)=−f(x)f(-x)=-f(x)f(−x)=−f(x)
  4. asymptotes
    • VA approach both side to aaa where f(a)f(a)f(a) DNE
    • SA
      • y=mx+by=mx+by=mx+b
        m=lim⁡x→±∞f(x)xm=\lim\limits_{x\to\pm\infty}\frac{f(x)}xm=x→±∞lim​xf(x)​
        b=lim⁡x→±∞(f−mx)b=\lim\limits_{x\to\pm\infty}(f-mx)b=x→±∞lim​(f−mx)
    • (only if m=0m=0m=0) HA approach to −∞-\infty−∞ and ∞\infty∞
    • do HA=f(x)\text{HA}=f(x)HA=f(x) to see if intersection exists
  5. cp ip
    • use f′(x)=0f'(x)=0f′(x)=0 to get cps
    • use f′′(x)=0f''(x)=0f′′(x)=0 to get ips
  6. test signs
    • use f(x)f(x)f(x) to test + or - on intervals divided by y-int and VA
    • use f′(x)f'(x)f′(x) to test ↗ or ↘ (divided by cps)
    • use f′′(x)f''(x)f′′(x) to test ∪ or ∩ (divided by ips)
  7. draw graph

Example

f(x)=x5−15x3=x3(x−15)(x+15)f′(x)=5x4=5x2(x+3)(x−3)f′′(x)=20x3−90x=10x(2x−3)(2x+3)\begin{aligned} f(x)&=x^5-15x^3=x^3(x-\sqrt{15})(x+\sqrt{15})\\ f'(x)&=5x^4=5x^2(x+3)(x-3)\\ f''(x)&=20x^3-90x=10x(\sqrt2x-3)(\sqrt2x+3) \end{aligned}f(x)f′(x)f′′(x)​=x5−15x3=x3(x−15​)(x+15​)=5x4=5x2(x+3)(x−3)=20x3−90x=10x(2​x−3)(2​x+3)​

  1. polynomial ∴Dom(f)=R\therefore\text{Dom}(f)=\mathbb{R}∴Dom(f)=R
  2. f(x)=0f(x)=0f(x)=0 when x=0x=0x=0 and x=±15≈±3.87x=\pm\sqrt{15}\approx\pm3.87x=±15​≈±3.87
    f(0)=0f(0)=0f(0)=0
  3. ∵f(−x)=(−x)5−15(−x)3=−x5+15x3=−f(x)\because f(-x)=(-x)^5-15(-x)^3=-x^5+15x^3=-f(x)∵f(−x)=(−x)5−15(−x)3=−x5+15x3=−f(x)
    ∴f(x)\therefore f(x)∴f(x) is odd
    ∴\therefore∴ only need to consider [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞)
  4. ∵Dom(f)=R∴\because\text{Dom}(f)=\mathbb{R}\quad\therefore∵Dom(f)=R∴ no VA
    ∵lim⁡x→∞f(x)=∞∴\because\lim\limits_{x\to\infty}f(x)=\infty\quad\therefore∵x→∞lim​f(x)=∞∴ no HA
    no SA
  5. f′(x)=0f'(x)=0f′(x)=0 when x=0x=0x=0 and x=±3x=\pm3x=±3
    f′′(x)=0f''(x)=0f′′(x)=0 when x=0x=0x=0 and x=±32≈±2.12x=\pm\frac3{\sqrt2}\approx\pm2.12x=±2​3​≈±2.12
  6. (test values, then) chart
       0             2.12     3    3.87
───┼────────────────────────────┼────────>
   │f(1) < 0                    │f(4) > 0
f  │             neg            │  pos
───┼──────────────────────┼─────┴────────
   │f'(1) < 0             │f(4) > 0      
f' │          ↘          min      ↗
───┼──────────────┼───────┴──────────────
   │f''(1) < 0    │f''(3) > 0
f''│       ∩      IP          ∪
───┴──────────────┴──────────────────────

                           🭿
0 ─── 2.12 ─── 3 ─── 3.87 ───>
   🭾        🭼     🭿
  7. draw graph (with left side mirrored)

Theorems

EVT

if fff is continuous on [a,b][a,b][a,b], then fff has an abs max and an abs min at [a,b][a,b][a,b]

Rolle

if fff is continuous on [a,b][a,b][a,b] and diffable on (a,b)(a,b)(a,b) and f(a)=f(b)=0f(a)=f(b)=0f(a)=f(b)=0, then ∃c∈(a,b)f′(c)=0\exists c\in(a,b)\enspace f'(c)=0∃c∈(a,b)f′(c)=0

MVT

if fff is continuous on [a,b][a,b][a,b] and diffable on (a,b)(a,b)(a,b), then

∃c∈(a,b)f(b)−f(a)b−a=f′(c)\textcolor{#66ccff}{\exists c\in(a,b)\quad\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}=f'(c)} ∃c∈(a,b)b−af(b)−f(a)​=f′(c)

Infimum and Supremum

  • for finite sets
    • Inf S=min{Sn}\text{Inf }S=\text{min}\{S_n\}Inf S=min{Sn​}
    • Sup S=max{Sn}\text{Sup }S=\text{max}\{S_n\}Sup S=max{Sn​}
  • for infinite sets
    • Inf S=lim⁡n→−∞Sn\text{Inf }S=\lim\limits_{n\to-\infty}S_nInf S=n→−∞lim​Sn​
    • Sup S=lim⁡n→∞Sn\text{Sup }S=\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}S_nSup S=n→∞lim​Sn​

Axioms

every nonempty set of real numbers that is bounded from above has a Supremum
every nonempty set of real numbers that is bounded from below has a Infimum

Sup-ε Theorem

let M=Sup SM=\text{Sup }SM=Sup S and ε>0∴  ∃s∈S st M−ε<s≤M\varepsilon>0\quad\therefore\;\exist s\in S\text{ st }M-\varepsilon<s\le Mε>0∴∃s∈S st M−ε<s≤M

  • proof

    ∵  Sup S=M∴  s≤Massume ¬∃s∈S st M−ε<s∴  ∀s∈Ss≤M−ε∴  Sup S=M−εcontradicts the initial assumption that Sup S=M∴  ∃s∈S st M−ε<s≤M\begin{aligned} &\because\;\text{Sup }S=M\\ &\therefore\;s\le M \end{aligned}\\ \begin{aligned} \text{assume }&\neg\exist s\in S\text{ st }M-\varepsilon<s\\ \therefore\;&\forall s\in S\enspace s\le M-\varepsilon\\ \therefore\;&\text{Sup }S=M-\varepsilon\\ &\text{contradicts the initial assumption that }\text{Sup }S=M\\ \therefore\;\exist s\in&S\text{ st }M-\varepsilon<s\le M \end{aligned}​∵Sup S=M∴s≤M​assume ∴∴∴∃s∈​¬∃s∈S st M−ε<s∀s∈Ss≤M−εSup S=M−εcontradicts the initial assumption that Sup S=MS st M−ε<s≤M​

Inf-ε Theorem

let m=Inf Sm=\text{Inf }Sm=Inf S and ε>0∴  ∃s∈S st m≤s<m+ε\varepsilon>0\quad\therefore\;\exist s\in S\text{ st }m\le s<m+\varepsilonε>0∴∃s∈S st m≤s<m+ε

  • proof

    ∵  Inf S=m∴  s≥massume ¬∃s∈S st s<m+ε∴  ∀s∈Sm+ε≤s∴  Inf S=m+εcontradicts the initial assumption that Inf S=M∴  ∃s∈S st m≤s<m+ε\begin{aligned} &\because\;\text{Inf }S=m\\ &\therefore\;s\ge m \end{aligned}\\ \begin{aligned} \text{assume }&\neg\exist s\in S\text{ st }s<m+\varepsilon\\ \therefore\;&\forall s\in S\enspace m+\varepsilon\le s\\ \therefore\;&\text{Inf }S=m+\varepsilon\\ &\text{contradicts the initial assumption that }\text{Inf }S=M\\ \therefore\;\exist s\in&S\text{ st }m\le s<m+\varepsilon \end{aligned}\\ ​∵Inf S=m∴s≥m​assume ∴∴∴∃s∈​¬∃s∈S st s<m+ε∀s∈Sm+ε≤sInf S=m+εcontradicts the initial assumption that Inf S=MS st m≤s<m+ε​

Exp

lim⁡x→cex=ec\lim\limits_{x\to c}e^x=e^cx→clim​ex=ec

  • prerequisite

    lim⁡h→0(1+h)1h=lim⁡n→∞(1+1n)n=elim⁡h→0eh−1h=1\begin{aligned} \lim\limits_{h\to0}(1+h)^{\frac1h}&=\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}(1+\frac1n)^n=e\\ \lim\limits_{h\to0}\frac{e^h-1}h&=1 \end{aligned}h→0lim​(1+h)h1​h→0lim​heh−1​​=n→∞lim​(1+n1​)n=e=1​

  • proof

    lim⁡x→cex=lim⁡h→0eh+c=lim⁡h→0ec⋅lim⁡h→0eh=eclim⁡h→0(eh−1h⋅h+1)=ec(1⋅0+1)=ec■\begin{aligned} \lim\limits_{x\to c}e^x&=\lim\limits_{h\to0}e^{h+c}=\lim\limits_{h\to0}e^c\cdot\lim\limits_{h\to0}e^h=e^c\lim\limits_{h\to0}\left(\frac{e^h-1}h\cdot h+1\right)\\ &=e^c(1\cdot0+1)=e^c\quad\blacksquare \end{aligned}x→clim​ex​=h→0lim​eh+c=h→0lim​ec⋅h→0lim​eh=ech→0lim​(heh−1​⋅h+1)=ec(1⋅0+1)=ec■​

Sandwich

lim⁡x→cg(x)≤lim⁡x→cf(x)≤lim⁡x→ch(x)∧lim⁡x→cg(x)=lim⁡x→ch(x)=L  ⟹  lim⁡x→cf(x)=L\begin{aligned}&\textcolor{aqua}{\lim\limits_{x\to c}g(x)\le\textcolor{coral}{\lim\limits_{x\to c}f(x)}\le\lim\limits_{x\to c}h(x)}\land\textcolor{lime}{\lim\limits_{x\to c}g(x)=\lim\limits_{x\to c}h(x)=L}\\\implies&\lim\limits_{x\to c}f(x)=L\end{aligned} ⟹​x→clim​g(x)≤x→clim​f(x)≤x→clim​h(x)∧x→clim​g(x)=x→clim​h(x)=Lx→clim​f(x)=L​

Assume x3

Frac Upper
